office_does: A U.S. Representative writes and votes on federal legislation, controls federal spending through appropriations, conducts oversight of the executive branch, and provides constituent services for the district.
race_blurb: Incumbent Pramila Jayapal (D), first elected in 2016, faces three first-time federal challengers in one of the safest Democratic seats in the country. Under the top-two primary, the practical question is which challenger advances with her to the November ballot.

candidates.summary: Incumbent U.S. Representative since 2017 and former Washington State Senator; she chairs emerita the Congressional Progressive Caucus and is Ranking Member of the House Immigration Subcommittee.
highlights: Lead sponsor of the Medicare for All Act, framing universal healthcare as a right [S1]
Has sponsored 193 pieces of legislation over nine years in the House, including the Ultra-Millionaire Tax Act and ICE Out of Our Faces Act [S3]
Founded and led the immigrant-advocacy organization OneAmerica for 12 years before elected office [S2]
States she has never taken corporate PAC money and leads bills to ban congressional stock trading [S1]
Endorsed by the Washington State Labor Council, Planned Parenthood, Sierra Club, and King County Democrats, among others [S1]

taxes.basis: Backs an ultra-millionaire wealth tax in her pamphlet statement and sponsored the Ultra-Millionaire Tax Act of 2026 (H.R. 8085).

spending.basis: Platform and sponsored bills center on expanding federal programs: Medicare for All, tuition-free college, and housing guaranteed as a human right.

safety.basis: Sponsored the Housing not Handcuffs Act of 2025 (H.R. 4182), pointing away from criminalization and toward housing/services responses.

climate.basis: States she fights climate change in pamphlet and campaign materials, though the dossier records no specific climate legislation.

experience.basis: Nine years in the House with 193 sponsored bills, committee assignments, and Progressive Caucus leadership; the only candidate with a legislative record.

reform.basis: Says she has never taken corporate PAC money; leads bills to ban congressional stock trading and supports Supreme Court and campaign-finance reform.

immigration.basis: OneAmerica founder who campaigns against ICE/CBP enforcement actions and sponsored the ICE Out of Our Faces Act and Dignity for Detained Immigrants Act.

healthcare.basis: Lead sponsor of the Medicare for All Act, framing universal healthcare as a right, not a privilege.

social.basis: Co-chairs the Trans Equality Task Force and sponsored a resolution supporting a Transgender Bill of Rights.

defense.basis: Authored an Iran War Powers resolution and a bill to sunset the 2001 AUMF, but voted for Iron Dome funding despite a generally pro-Palestinian stance.

candidates.summary: Political and labor organizer and grocery worker who moved to Washington from Missouri in 2023; she is a first-time candidate running under a Democratic Party preference.
highlights: Grocery worker and union strike captain who organized coworkers against corporate grocery mergers [S1]
Supports universal healthcare, free education, and publicly owned utilities and grocery stores [S1]
Runs explicitly against the Democratic establishment, saying her aim is to take over the Democratic Party first [S1]

taxes.basis: Frames her candidacy around fighting billionaires and public ownership, implying but not stating a progressive-revenue position.

spending.basis: Pamphlet platform of universal healthcare, free education for all, and publicly owned utilities and grocery stores is a maximal public-investment program.

experience.basis: No elected or governmental record; runs on labor-organizing experience and explicitly against incumbent leadership.

reform.basis: Says the Democratic Party has zero interest in fighting the billionaires and frames incumbent leadership as failed, though via taking over the party rather than structural reforms.

healthcare.basis: Supports universal healthcare and says she has personally knocked thousands of doors for it.

social.basis: Attributes her move to Washington to escaping anti-transgender discrimination and seeking healthcare access; no explicit policy platform on social issues.

candidates.summary: First-time candidate running under a Republican Party preference; a Marine veteran and former Lakewood police officer who immigrated from India and now runs family businesses in Pierce County.
highlights: Campaigns on bringing law and order back to Seattle and funding law enforcement and emergency services [S1]
Marine veteran (2011-2014) and former Lakewood police officer [S1]
Supports energy independence and investment in electric-grid resilience [S2]
Promotes legal immigration pathways; a news column reports he has posted content opposing birthright citizenship [S4]

spending.basis: Promises military-grade accountability in Washington, D.C. and careful stewardship of tax dollars, while also backing infrastructure and public-safety funding.

safety.basis: Lead campaign theme is bringing law and order back to Seattle, with support for funding law enforcement, drawing on his own policing background.

climate.basis: Cites energy costs as a breaking point for the district and campaigns on energy independence rather than climate policy.

experience.basis: No prior elected office; runs on military, policing, and small-business experience rather than a governmental record.

immigration.basis: Promotes legal-pathway immigration on his website while a news column reports he has posted content opposing birthright citizenship.

candidates.summary: Perennial Seattle-area candidate running under the Fifth Republic Party preference; a former wildlife biologist who has run unsuccessfully for school board and governor and holds no elected office.
highlights: Platform is centered on explicitly antisemitic conspiracy claims in his pamphlet statement and campaign website [S1]
Perennial candidate: school board runs in 1999, 2007, and 2015 and gubernatorial runs in 2016 and 2020, all unsuccessful [S3]
Calls the environment his number-one issue, with an Earth First theme on his website [S2]
Proposes downsizing the military to fund free healthcare and cutting all ties with Israel [S1]

climate.basis: Calls the environment his number-one issue and puts the natural world before corporate profits, though without specific climate or energy legislation proposals.

experience.basis: Has never held elected office across repeated runs since 1999 and submitted no elected or professional experience to the pamphlet.

healthcare.basis: Proposes free healthcare, funded by downsizing the military.

defense.basis: Proposes downsizing the military and cutting all ties with and derecognizing Israel, positions embedded in his broader antisemitic platform.
